title: Judicial activism in the Philippines

audience_match: Philippine judges and justices at all court levels
Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. (subject of the tribute portion)
Inquirer Opinion readers following the US-PH judicial-philosophy comparison
Law students preparing for the bar — particularly on Article VIII Section 1 doctrine

primary_topics: Constitutional comparison of US and Philippine approaches to judicial activism — and why the Philippine Constitution makes activism a DUTY, not a choice

Article VIII Section 1 of the 1987 Constitution as the explicit textual basis for judicial activism: 'duty of the courts of justice... to determine whether or not there has been a grave abuse of discretion'
The two-part structure of judicial power: ordinary review (settling actual controversies) + extraordinary/expanded certiorari (grave abuse of discretion)
Reading the second impeachment complaint case against then-CJ Davide (Francisco v. House of Representatives, November 10, 2003) as the paradigmatic example of expanded-certiorari activism
The libertarian dimension: Philippine activism is doubly mandated by Bill of Rights protection of civil liberties
Personal tribute to Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. — recently retired RTC Naga City judge whose forthcoming book is titled 'Judicial Activist'
sub_topics: Continuation of CJP's previous Monday column on US judicial debate

Two parts of Article VIII Section 1: (1) ordinary judicial review — 'settle actual controversies involving rights which are legally demandable and enforceable'; (2) extraordinary expanded certiorari — 'determine whether or not there has been a grave abuse of discretion amounting to lack or excess of jurisdiction'
'Duty' (not 'power' or 'authority') — duty cannot be evaded under any circumstance
Separation of powers — three branches supreme in their own spheres
Limits: judiciary cannot interfere with Congress's INTERNAL rules of lawmaking or the Presidency's INTERNAL confidential orders
Exception: when the Constitution imposes 'conditions, limitations, or restrictions' on the exercise of legislative or executive power, the judiciary may review for compliance with those conditions

Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003) — second impeachment complaint against CJ Davide; constitutional ban on more than one impeachment proceeding 'against the same official... within a period of one year' treated as a 'condition or limitation'; House obeyed 'after several days that tested its adherence to the rule of law'
US Supreme Court libertarian-jurisprudence references: 'separate is not equal' (Brown), 'read him his rights' (Miranda), 'one-man-one-vote', 'fruit of the poisonous tree'
Philippine judges anchor libertarianism on 'impregnable Constitution' — US judges anchor on jurisprudence (changeable)

Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. — Regional Trial Court of Naga City (recently retired); previously written about by CJP on January 21, 2018, October 11, 2020, and November 8, 2020 ('I have never written about any trial judge as many times')
Judge Santos's forthcoming book 'Judicial Activist'; CJP requested to write the Foreword
Personal connection: both CJP and Judge Santos were student activists

CJP as youngest president of FEU Central Student Organization and co-founder/president of National Union of Students of the Philippines (NUSP) — 'the largest student organization in our country since then up to now'
Mild regret: Judge Santos 'did not get to the Supreme Court where his decisions could have added wisdom and luster to the Philippine Reports and the Supreme Court Reports Annotated'

signature_phrases.phrase: by imposing upon all judges the duty to determine issues of grave abuse of discretion, our Constitution has thereby mandated them to be activists
Power can be relinquished, but a duty cannot under any circumstance be evaded
the three great branches of our government... are supreme in their own spheres
tested its adherence to the rule of law
American magistrates are accorded the luxury of choosing between being passivists or activists
This luxury is denied to all Philippine judges
anchor their libertarianism on jurisprudence that can be changed anytime
could have added wisdom and luster
Philippine Reports and the Supreme Court Reports Annotated
the largest student organization in our country since then up to now

Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. (retired RTC Naga City judge; author of forthcoming 'Judicial Activist')
institutions: Regional Trial Court of Naga City
National Union of Students of the Philippines (NUSP)
Philippine Reports / Supreme Court Reports Annotated
cases: Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003) — second impeachment complaint against CJ Davide; activated expanded certiorari to enforce the one-year impeachment-ban condition
(Implicitly cited US cases) Brown v. Board ('separate is not equal'), Miranda v. Arizona ('read him his rights'), one-man-one-vote, fruit of the poisonous tree
laws_treaties: 1987 Philippine Constitution — Article VIII Section 1 (judicial power, duty to determine grave abuse)
1987 Philippine Constitution — Article XI (impeachment, one-year ban)
events: Judge Santos's recent retirement from RTC Naga City
Upcoming release of Judge Santos's book 'Judicial Activist'

stances.claim: Article VIII Section 1 of the 1987 Constitution makes judicial activism a constitutional DUTY for all Philippine judges — not a discretionary philosophical leaning as in the United States. By imposing upon all judges 'the duty to determine issues of grave abuse of discretion,' the Constitution has 'mandated them to be activists.' Power can be relinquished; duty cannot.
stances.rhetorical_move: constitutional-textual-mandate argument distinguishing PH from US

stances.claim: Judicial power under the 1987 Constitution has two parts: ORDINARY judicial review of actual controversies, and EXTRAORDINARY (or 'expanded certiorari') review for grave abuse of discretion amounting to lack or excess of jurisdiction. The second part is what makes Philippine activism mandatory.
stances.rhetorical_move: two-part textual exegesis as analytical scaffolding

stances.claim: The judiciary cannot interfere with Congress's INTERNAL lawmaking rules or with the Presidency's INTERNAL confidential orders — these are the limits of separation of powers. HOWEVER, when the Constitution imposes 'conditions, limitations, or restrictions' on legislative or executive power (e.g., the one-year impeachment ban), the judiciary MAY review for compliance with those conditions. Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003) is the paradigm: the SC restrained the House from continuing the second impeachment complaint against CJ Davide within the prohibited one-year period.

Philippine judges must be both activist AND libertarian — the Bill of Rights replicates US libertarian jurisprudence (Brown, Miranda, one-man-one-vote, fruit of the poisonous tree) but anchors that libertarianism on the 'impregnable Constitution' rather than jurisprudence that can be changed anytime. American magistrates have the 'luxury' of choosing between passivism and activism; Philippine judges do not.
stances.confidence: asserted as the speaker's settled jurisprudential reading

notable_anecdotes.summary: Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003) — the House, after the SC restrained continuation of the second impeachment complaint, 'after several days that tested its adherence to the rule of law' obeyed 'reluctantly' — a paradigmatic test of separation-of-powers discipline.

Personal soulmate-disclosure: Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. 'brought to the bench his activism during his student days' — and CJP, as a soulmate, was also a student activist, serving as the youngest president of the FEU Central Student Organization and as a co-founder and president of NUSP, 'the largest student organization in our country since then up to now.'

CJP has written about Judge Santos 'at least three times' (January 21, 2018; October 11, 2020; November 8, 2020) and notes: 'I have never written about any trial judge as many times and in as many subjects as I have about him.'

Mild regret that Judge Santos 'did not get to the Supreme Court where his decisions could have added wisdom and luster to the Philippine Reports and the Supreme Court Reports Annotated' — partially comforted by the forthcoming book 'Judicial Activist' for which CJP was asked to write the Foreword.

decision_framework_signals: treats Article VIII Section 1 expanded-certiorari clause as imposing duty (non-relinquishable), not power (relinquishable)
uses the internal-vs-conditional distinction to allocate judicial interference appropriately
applies condition-or-limitation analysis (Francisco v. House paradigm) to questions of constitutional separation
treats the Bill of Rights as the foundation for mandatory libertarian jurisprudence
uses comparative-constitutionalism to elevate PH activism from optional to obligatory
frames tribute-within-doctrine as a recurring CJP technique — honoring a colleague while making a constitutional point

one_paragraph_summary: Published April 10, 2023 as a follow-up to CJP's previous Monday column on the US judicial-activism debate, this piece argues that the 1987 Philippine Constitution makes activism a DUTY rather than a philosophical choice. Article VIII Section 1 grants judicial power in two parts: ORDINARY review of actual controversies, and EXTRAORDINARY (expanded certiorari) review of grave abuse of discretion amounting to lack or excess of jurisdiction. By imposing the second as a DUTY ('not just a power or authority'), the Constitution has 'mandated [judges] to be activists' — and 'a duty cannot under any circumstance be evaded.' The judiciary cannot interfere with Congress's INTERNAL lawmaking rules or with confidential executive orders, but when the Constitution imposes 'conditions, limitations, or restrictions' (such as the one-year ban on repeat impeachment proceedings), the judiciary may enforce them. The paradigm is Francisco v. House of Representatives (November 10, 2003): the SC restrained the House from continuing the second impeachment complaint against CJ Hilario G. Davide Jr. — and 'after several days that tested its adherence to the rule of law,' the House 'reluctantly' obeyed. Philippine judges must additionally be libertarian: the Bill of Rights replicates US libertarian jurisprudence (Brown's 'separate is not equal,' Miranda's 'read him his rights,' one-man-one-vote, fruit of the poisonous tree) but anchors it on the 'impregnable Constitution' rather than changeable jurisprudence. 'American magistrates are accorded the luxury of choosing between being passivists or activists in their philosophical leaning. This luxury is denied to all Philippine judges.' The column closes with a personal tribute to recently-retired Judge Soliman M. Santos Jr. of RTC Naga City — a 'soulmate' fellow-student-activist whose forthcoming book is titled 'Judicial Activist' and for which CJP was asked to write the Foreword. CJP recalls his own student activism (youngest president of FEU Central Student Organization; co-founder/president of NUSP, 'the largest student organization in our country since then up to now') and gently regrets that Judge Santos did not reach the Supreme Court.
